perturbation.original_text: 1. Confidential Information; Representatives. (a) The Receiving Party has indicated interest in cooperation possibilities, and, in order to assist the Receiving Party in evaluating such cooperation, the Company is prepared to make available to the Receiving Party certain information concerning the business, operations, strategy and prospects of the Company (all such information, the “ Confidential Information ”).
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Confidential Information; Representatives. (a) The Receiving Party has indicated interest in cooperation possibilities, and, in order to assist the Receiving Party in evaluating such cooperation, the Company is prepared to make available to the Receiving Party certain information concerning the business, operations, strategy and prospects of the Company (all such information, the “Public Information”).
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Confidential Information" to "Public Information" contradicts the intent of the agreement, which is to protect sensitive data. The term "Public Information" has a specific legal meaning, referring to data freely accessible to anyone, directly opposing the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: FOIA defines public information as accessible to anyone. This directly contradicts the agreement's purpose of protecting sensitive information, making the agreement unenforceable if the information is labeled "Public Information."
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Office of Information Policy | The Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552 Skip to main content The Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552 Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email Below is the full text of the Freedom of Information Act in a form showing all amendments to the statute made by the “FOIA Improvement Act of 2016." All newly enacted provisions in boldface type replace the strikethrough text. Full Text of the FOIA Improvement Act of 2016 (Public Law No. 114-185) § 552. Public information; agency rules, opinions, orders, records, and proceedings (a) Each agency shall make available to the public information as follows: (1) Each agency shall separately state and currently publish in the Federal Register for the guidance of the public— (A) descriptions of its central and field organization and the established places at which, the employees (and in the case of a uniformed service, the members) from whom, and the methods whereby, the public may obtain information, make submittals or requests, or obtain decisions; (B) statements of the general course and method by which its functions are channeled and determined, including the nature and requirements of all formal and informal procedures available; (C) rules of procedure, descriptions of forms available or the places at which forms may be obtained, and instructions as to the scope and contents of all papers, reports, or examinations; (D) substantive rules of general applicability adopted as authorized by law, and statements of general policy or interpretations of general applicability formulated and adopted by the agency; and (E) each amendment, revision, or repeal of the foregoing. Except to the extent that a person has actual and timely notice of the terms thereof, a person may not in any manner be required to resort to, or be adversely affected by, a matter required to be published in the Federal Register and not so published. For the purpose of this paragraph, matter reasonably available to the class of persons affected thereby is deemed published in the Federal Register when incorpor
perturbation.justification: The change from "Confidential Information" to "Public Information" creates a direct contradiction. The original text aims to protect sensitive data, while the changed text redefines it as information freely accessible to the public. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation confirm this. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining 'Public information' under the Freedom of Information Act, supports the contradiction because FOIA mandates agencies to make public information accessible. This directly opposes the confidentiality intended in the original contract, making the modified term legally contradictory and likely unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Limitations on Use and Disclosure of Confidential Information. (a) The Receiving Party shall, and shall direct its Representatives to, keep the Confidential Information in confidence and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however, that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information to which the Company gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible cooperation and who agree to keep such information in confidence to the same extent as described herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Limitations on Use and Disclosure of Confidential Information. (a) The Receiving Party shall, and shall direct its Representatives to, use the Confidential Information at their own discretion and may disclose the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however, that (i) the Receiving Party must make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information if they receive written consent from any 3rd party, and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Information must be disclosed to the general public.
perturbation.explanation: The modification drastically alters the confidentiality obligations. Original text restricts disclosure, change allows it. This contradicts the core purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement. It introduces uncertainty regarding enforcement and the nature of the confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act protects businesses' trade secrets. This change directly contradicts this protection by allowing the disclosure of confidential information, potentially leading to the loss of trade secret protection for the company.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text fundamentally contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and potentially violates trade secret protections. The original text mandates keeping Confidential Information in confidence, while the changed text allows the Receiving Party to use and disclose it at their discretion and even requires disclosure to the general public or any third party with written consent. The Law Explanation states that this directly contradicts the protection provided by the Trade Secrets Act and could result in losing trade secret protection for the company. While the provided snippets primarily address civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation (18 USC 1836) and list the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, the core principle of protecting confidential information is severely undermined by the changed text's allowance and mandate of disclosure. This represents a clear legal contradiction as it reverses the obligation to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Return of Confidential Information. Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return or destroy, at its sole option, all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect to a substantial degree any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Return of Confidential Information. Upon the Company's request, the Receiving Party may keep, or share with public, all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect to a substantial degree any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "return or destroy, at its sole option" to "may keep, or share with public" directly opposes the purpose of the Non-Disclosure Agreement. This alteration creates ambiguity regarding the ownership and control of confidential information, making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets by requiring those who misappropriate them to return them to their rightful owner. This modification directly contradicts this principle by allowing the receiving party to keep and share the information publicly, thus misappropriating the trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text fundamentally contradicts the purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA), as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The original text requires the Receiving Party to return or destroy confidential information, whereas the changed text allows them to keep or share it publicly. Although the scraped law snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation states that the UTSA requires those who misappropriate trade secrets to return them. By allowing the receiving party to keep and share the information publicly, the modified text directly enables misappropriation, a blatant violation of the principles underlying trade secret protection and NDAs.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Korea, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to North Korea makes the agreement almost impossible to enforce in a practical sense, as North Korean legal system is not accessible to most international parties and does not adhere to international legal norms. This introduces significant uncertainty and renders the enforcement of the agreement highly improbable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Comity and Public Policy
perturbation.law_explanation: US Courts can refuse to enforce the laws of another jurisdiction based on comity and public policy. Given the vast differences in legal systems and human rights issues between the US and North Korea, a court is highly unlikely to enforce a contract governed by North Korean law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law from New York to North Korea. The Perturbation Explanation states this change makes the agreement almost impossible to enforce due to the inaccessibility of North Korea's legal system and its non-adherence to international legal norms. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that US courts can refuse to enforce the laws of another jurisdiction based on comity and public policy, especially considering the vast differences in legal systems and human rights issues between the US and North Korea. While there are no usable scraped snippets, the explanations provided highlight a direct conflict with the principle of enforceability and comity, effectively nullifying the legal intent of the governing law clause. Therefore, the modification results in clear non-compliance by rendering the contract virtually unenforceable in any reasonable jurisdiction. This is a direct contradiction to the implied requirement that a contract be governed by an accessible and recognized legal system.
